results.possibleIdentity: Denys George Irving (1944–1976), also known as Dennis Irving, was a Welsh filmmaker, computer art pioneer, and musician who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is recognized for his early experimentation with computer-generated films and multimedia projection systems. While a graduate student at Columbia University in New York City during the late 1960s, he utilized the university's mainframe computers to create short, computer-generated silent films such as '69' and 'Now'. During this time in New York, he also developed 'psychedelic' projection systems using liquid inks and photographic slides, which he used to project images onto buildings in the East Village. In 1968, he recorded an interview with the experimental jazz composer Sun Ra for Pacifica Radio, where he was credited as Dennis Irving. Upon returning to London in 1969, he founded Lucifer Films Ltd. and later Lucifer Recordings. Under the moniker 'Lucifer', he released several records in the early 1970s, including the LPs 'Big Gun' and 'Exit', for which he performed all instruments and vocals. His work as a filmmaker and animator is held in the LUX collection and the British Film Institute (BFI) archive, and his computer art has been exhibited at the Tate Gallery. His technological approach to art and his presence in the New York avant-garde scene during the late 1960s align with the experimental goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

results.possibleIdentity: Richard Schulman is a prominent American photographer known for his architectural photography and portraits of influential figures in the art and architecture worlds. Active since the early 1970s (and possibly the late 1960s), he has photographed legendary artists such as Andy Warhol and Jean-Michel Basquiat, as well as renowned architects like Philip Johnson, Frank Gehry, and Zaha Hadid. His association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is supported by archival records at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), which list him in connection with the organization's projects, such as the Pepsi-Cola Pavilion at Expo '70. Schulman has described receiving early commissions from scientists (including one developing military lasers), which reflects the interdisciplinary spirit of E.A.T. and the collaborations it fostered between artists and engineers. His work is housed in major archives like Getty Images (formerly Corbis) and has been featured in publications such as The New York Times, Vogue, and The New Yorker.

Richard Schulman (also known as Commander Richard Schulman) was a pioneer in the field of computer art and graphics during the 1970s. A retired U.S. Naval commander and software developer, he was involved in early experiments with computer-generated imagery and led project subcommittees in computer graphics. He is known to have exhibited serigraphs created through computer technology, such as in the 1978 exhibition featured in the journal 'Computer Graphics and Art'. His work often involved collaborations with engineers from Bell Labs, specifically Kenneth Knowlton, who was a key figure in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. This identity aligns closely with the E.A.T. mission of merging artistic practice with scientific and engineering expertise during the late 1960s and 1970s.

results.possibleIdentity: Frank Kuenstler (April 17, 1928 – August 11, 1996) was a prominent American avant-garde poet, filmmaker, and visual artist active in New York City from the 1950s through the 1990s. He was a central figure in the 'Eventorium,' an arts collective on Manhattan's Upper West Side that he co-founded in the mid-1960s with Hunter Ingalls. Kuenstler is historically significant for his early association with the Fluxus movement; his 1961 reading series 'Bread & AG' at the AG Gallery is recognized by some scholars as the first official Fluxus event. His artistic output was diverse, including experimental films such as 'Color Idioms' (1968) and 'El Atlantis' (1973), and 'wall sculptures' made from wood assemblages and found objects. In literature, he is best known for 'LENS' (1964), a radical experimental poem published by Film Culture. His work is often characterized by linguistic play, puns, and the intersection of visual and aural elements. He also edited several small-press magazines, including 'Eventorium Muse,' 'Bread&,' and 'Airplane,' and taught at the School of Visual Arts. While Kuenstler himself is often linked to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) through his close collaborator Hunter Ingalls and his inclusion in relevant avant-garde catalogs like 'The Arts in Fusion' (1966), his primary legacy remains in the realms of experimental poetry and independent film. He died in Manhattan in 1996.

results.possibleIdentity: David Rothenberg (born 1933) is a prominent New York-based theatrical producer, press agent, and social activist who was highly active during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Originally from New Jersey, he became a successful press agent for Broadway and Off-Broadway productions, representing figures such as Edward Albee, Alvin Ailey, Elizabeth Taylor, and Bette Davis. His career took a pivotal turn in 1967 when he produced the controversial play 'Fortune and Men’s Eyes' by John Herbert, which depicted the harsh realities of prison life. Following the play's success and the ensuing public discussions with former inmates, Rothenberg founded The Fortune Society in 1967, a non-profit organization dedicated to prison reform and helping formerly incarcerated individuals reintegrate into society. He was an observer during the Attica prison riot in 1971 and has hosted a long-running radio program on WBAI-FM in New York for decades. While primarily known for theatre and activism, his role as a leading press agent in the New York avant-garde and performing arts scene placed him at the center of the cultural environment where organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) operated.

David Rothenberg (born 1962) is an American musician (primarily a jazz clarinetist), philosopher, and author known for his work at the intersection of music, technology, and nature. He is a Distinguished Professor of Philosophy and Music at the New Jersey Institute of Technology (NJIT). Although he was not active as a professional in the 1960s, his scholarly and artistic work frequently focuses on the history of art-science-technology collaborations and 'interspecies music' (performing alongside animals such as birds, whales, and insects). He is the author of several books including 'Survival of the Beautiful: Art, Science, and Evolution' (2011), 'Why Birds Sing' (2005), and 'Hand’s End: Technology and the Limits of Nature' (1993). Rothenberg is often cited in contemporary literature regarding the history and legacy of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and its associated events like '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering,' as his philosophical work explores the technological extension of human and animal creativity. He has collaborated with experimental artists like Pauline Oliveros and Scanner and edited 'The Book of Music and Nature.'

results.possibleIdentity: Sharon Marcus was a musician and arts administrator active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, closely associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). She served as the Membership Secretary for E.A.T. during its most active period and was involved in the logistics and coordination of major projects, including the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. As a performer, she is recognized as a recorder player who collaborated with avant-garde composer David Behrman. She performed on the recorder in Behrman's notable experimental piece "Players with Circuits" (1966), which utilized interactive electronic systems. Her work bridging performance and technical arts administration exemplifies the interdisciplinary mission of the E.A.T. organization.

Sharon Marcus (1943–2026) was a prominent Portland-based tapestry and fiber artist, educator, and writer. Her professional practice spanned nearly four decades, during which she became an established figure in both the Pacific Northwest and international contemporary tapestry communities. Trained in anthropology and archaeology, her woven tapestries often explored landscape, architectural references, and the conceptual facets of human habitation. She served as the head of the Fibers Department and Liberal Arts Department at the Oregon College of Art and Craft (OSAC) and was a former editor and publisher of the International Tapestry Journal (ITNET). While she was primarily active from the late 1970s onward, she was influenced by the experimental art movements of the early 1970s and worked extensively with mixed materials such as linen and wire.
